lanes.beat: Openings, expansions, and the people behind {city}'s small-business economy

angles.default: How {businessName} fits into {city}'s changing small-business landscape
angles.launch: A new {industryLabel} bet on {city}: why {businessName} is opening now
angles.funding: What {businessName}'s new backing means for {industryLabel} in {city}
angles.hiring: {businessName} is adding jobs — what that signals about {city}'s economy

lanes.reason: Covers what's opening and growing in {city}; {businessName}'s story is squarely on that beat.

back.whyFits: Local business reporters need a steady stream of opening, growth, and jobs stories with named owners. A {industryLabel} business in {city} with a concrete milestone is core inventory for this lane.
back.leadWith: The business milestone and a number: square footage, jobs added, customers served, or dollars invested in {city}.
back.avoidLeading: Your product's features or a generic 'we're passionate about quality' framing — this lane wants economics and people, not marketing copy.
back.proofNeeded: A founder available for a 20-minute interview, one verifiable number, and a photo of the owner in the space.
back.coveragePattern: Short business-section profiles, weekly 'what's opening' roundups, and quarterly local-economy trend pieces.

subjectLines.default: {city} story idea: {businessName} and where {industryLabel} is headed locally
subjectLines.launch: Opening in {city}: {businessName} ({industryLabel}) — owner available this week
subjectLines.funding: Local funding news: {businessName} in {city} — details and founder access

back.pitch: Hi {firstName} — I run {businessName}, a {industryLabel} business in {city}, {state}. {storyHook} Happy to share numbers and make time for a quick interview if it's a fit for your beat.
back.followUp: One follow-up after 4–5 business days; mention any new concrete detail rather than just bumping the thread.

lanes.beat: Neighborhood stories, local institutions, and the people who hold {city} together

angles.default: {businessName} as a neighborhood fixture: the local story behind the storefront
angles.community: How {businessName} is showing up for {city} beyond the cash register
angles.reputation: The long game: how {businessName} earned its standing in {city}

lanes.reason: Community editors look for stories where a business is a character in {city}'s neighborhood life, not just a commercial actor.

back.whyFits: This lane covers belonging, not commerce. If {businessName} touches a neighborhood ritual, a local cause, or a familiar corner of {city}, the editor has a frame for it.
back.leadWith: The human or neighborhood element — a partnership, a tradition, a regular, a before-and-after of the block.
back.avoidLeading: Revenue, growth plans, or anything that reads like a press release about the company itself.
back.proofNeeded: A second voice (a neighbor, partner org, or longtime customer) willing to be quoted alongside you.
back.coveragePattern: Warm weekly features, 'around the neighborhood' columns, and recurring local-institution profiles.

subjectLines.community: {businessName} + {city}: a community story with two voices ready to talk

back.pitch: Hi {firstName} — {businessName} has been part of {city} life in a way I think fits your pages. {storyHook} I can connect you with neighbors and partners who are part of the story, not just me.
back.followUp: One gentle follow-up after a week; community desks plan further ahead than daily news.

lanes.beat: Where to eat, shop, and spend a Saturday in {city}

angles.default: The experience of {businessName}: what it's like to walk in the door in {city}
angles.launch: First look: what {businessName} is bringing to {city}'s {industryLabel} scene
angles.awareness: The {city} spot locals keep recommending: inside {businessName}

lanes.reason: Lifestyle writers sell the experience of {city} to readers — if {businessName} is a place people can visit, taste, or try, it qualifies.

back.whyFits: This lane runs on sensory detail and 'worth your weekend' recommendations. A visitable {industryLabel} spot in {city} with a distinct point of view gives the writer material.
back.leadWith: What a first-time visitor sees, tastes, or feels — your single most distinctive offering, named specifically.
back.avoidLeading: Your business plan or backstory before the experience; this lane buys atmosphere first, biography second.
back.proofNeeded: Three or four strong photos of the space and product, plus a standing invitation to visit unannounced.
back.coveragePattern: First-look writeups, seasonal guides, and 'best of {city}' roundups assembled weeks in advance.

subjectLines.launch: First look invite: {businessName}, now open in {city}

back.pitch: Hi {firstName} — {businessName} just might be a fit for your {city} coverage. {storyHook} Come by anytime, no staging; photos available if deadlines are tight.
back.followUp: Follow up once after a week, ideally pegged to a season or upcoming guide they're likely assembling.

lanes.beat: Operators, trends, and playbooks across the {industryLabel} sector

angles.default: What {businessName}'s approach says about where {industryLabel} is heading
angles.funding: An operator's view: how {businessName} put new capital to work

lanes.reason: Trade reporters want operators with a repeatable lesson — {businessName} can be the case study, with {city} as the backdrop.

back.whyFits: Trade outlets care about the 'how' other operators can copy. If {businessName} does one thing differently — pricing, staffing, sourcing, retention — that method is the story, not the business itself.
back.leadWith: The specific operational decision and its measurable result; trades love a before/after metric.
back.avoidLeading: Local color or community angles — this audience is national peers, not {city} readers.
back.proofNeeded: One concrete metric you're willing to publish, and comfort being challenged on it by a knowledgeable reporter.
back.coveragePattern: Operator Q&As, tactical how-we-did-it features, and trend pieces quoting three or four practitioners.

subjectLines.funding: {industryLabel} operator on the record: what {businessName} did with new funding

back.pitch: Hi {firstName} — I run {businessName} in {city}, {state}. {storyHook} I can walk through the numbers behind it and what I'd tell other {industryLabel} operators trying the same thing.
back.followUp: Follow up after 5–7 days; offer yourself as a quotable source for their trend pieces even if this pitch doesn't land.

lanes.beat: What's happening in {city} this week — listings, openings, and things to do

angles.default: {businessName}'s next public moment, packaged as a clean calendar listing
angles.event: {businessName}'s upcoming event as a ready-to-publish {city} listing
angles.launch: Opening day at {businessName} as a datable, listable {city} moment

lanes.reason: Calendar editors publish anything public, datable, and open to {city} readers — the bar is logistics, not narrative.

back.whyFits: This is the most mechanical lane: if {businessName} has a public event with a date, time, place, and price, it gets listed. No persuasion required — just complete information.
back.leadWith: The five facts in the first two lines: what, when, where, cost, and how to register or RSVP.
back.avoidLeading: Your story or mission — calendar editors skim for logistics and discard anything that makes them hunt.
back.proofNeeded: A finalized date and a public signup or info link; tentative events get skipped, not held.
back.coveragePattern: Weekly listings compiled on a hard deadline, usually 7–10 days before publication.
back.pitch: Hi {firstName} — submitting an event at {businessName} for your {city} calendar. {storyHook} Date, time, location, cost, and signup link are below in list form.
back.followUp: Don't follow up on a single listing; resubmit the next event instead and become a reliable recurring source.

lanes.beat: The daily-ish email {city} actually reads: openings, oddities, and useful local intel

angles.default: {businessName} as a two-paragraph item {city} readers forward to a friend
angles.awareness: The 'did you know this exists in {city}?' item, starring {businessName}
angles.community: A feel-good {city} item: {businessName} and the people around it

lanes.reason: Newsletter writers need a fresh local item every single day — a tight, forwardable nugget about {businessName} is genuinely useful to them.

back.whyFits: Newsletters run on volume and voice. They can't visit everyone, so a pre-shaped item about {businessName} — short, specific, slightly surprising — slots straight into tomorrow's send.
back.leadWith: The single most forwardable fact about {businessName}, written the way a friend would text it.
back.avoidLeading: Long backstory or attachments; if the writer can't grasp the item in ten seconds, it dies in the inbox.
back.proofNeeded: One crisp fact they can verify with a link or a phone call, and a photo that works small.
back.coveragePattern: Daily or twice-weekly sends with 4–6 short items; quick turnaround, short shelf life, frequent reader-tip callouts.

subjectLines.awareness: A {city} item your readers will forward: {businessName}

back.pitch: Hi {firstName} — short one for the newsletter: {storyHook} That's {businessName} in {city}. Happy to confirm details by phone in five minutes if useful.
back.followUp: Follow up after 3–4 days max — newsletter items go stale fast; offer a fresher angle if the first one aged out.

lanes.beat: Long-form conversations with the people building things in and around {city}

angles.default: The founder conversation: what building {businessName} in {city} actually took
angles.reputation: {businessName}'s founder on the decisions they'd defend and the ones they'd redo
angles.funding: Money talk, honestly: {businessName}'s founder on raising and spending in {city}

lanes.reason: Podcast hosts book guests, not stories — if the founder of {businessName} can talk candidly for 40 minutes, that's the product.

back.whyFits: This lane needs a person, not a press angle. A founder with opinions, a setback they'll discuss honestly, and a {city} connection gives the host an episode, not just a mention.
back.leadWith: One tension or hard call from your story — hosts book guests who sound like they'll say something unexpected.
back.avoidLeading: A polished company summary; if it sounds like a commercial, hosts assume the episode will too.
back.proofNeeded: A previous talk, a voice memo, or three sharp bullet takes — anything proving you're listenable for 40 minutes.
back.coveragePattern: Weekly or biweekly episodes booked 3–6 weeks out, with evergreen shelf life and show-notes links.

subjectLines.reputation: A guest with opinions: {businessName}'s founder on {industryLabel}'s hard calls

back.pitch: Hi {firstName} — I'm the founder of {businessName} in {city}, {state}. {storyHook} I'm happy to get into what went wrong along the way, not just the highlight reel. Three possible episode threads below.
back.followUp: Follow up after two weeks; booking calendars are long, so a slow reply usually means 'queued,' not 'no.'

lanes.beat: Morning-show segments and local features across {state}

angles.default: A three-minute visual segment: {businessName} as a {state} morning-show moment
angles.event: Live-from-{city} potential: {businessName}'s event as a broadcast backdrop
angles.community: A {state} feel-good segment: {businessName} and its {city} neighbors

lanes.reason: TV producers think in pictures — if something at {businessName} moves, demonstrates, or delights on camera, there's a segment in it.

back.whyFits: Television needs action the camera can follow. If {businessName} involves making, teaching, performing, or transforming something, a producer can storyboard it; if it's two people talking, they can't.
back.leadWith: The visual: describe exactly what the camera would see in the first ten seconds of the segment.
back.avoidLeading: Abstract claims about quality or service — if it can't be filmed, it can't lead a TV pitch.
back.proofNeeded: A short phone video of the visual moment, plus flexibility for early-morning or live-window filming.
back.coveragePattern: Recurring weekday segments planned days ahead, with last-minute slots when stories fall through.

subjectLines.default: Segment idea from {city}: {businessName} (strong visuals, flexible timing)
subjectLines.event: Filmable {city} event: {businessName} — visuals and timing inside

back.pitch: Hi {firstName} — segment idea from {city}: {storyHook} It's highly visual — I can send a 30-second phone clip so you can judge the pictures before committing.
back.followUp: Follow up after 3–4 days and again the week of any event; producers triage daily, so timing matters more than persistence.
